coredata.dc:title: Metal free, carbon-TiO2 based composites for the visible light photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Markad, Ganesh B.
Kapoor, Sudhir
Haram, Santosh K.
Thakur, Pragati
coredata.dc:description: Graphene-carbon nanodots-TiO2 novel ternary composite was developed as visible light photocatalyst. Efficiency and visible light sensitivity of TiO2 for the photodecoloration were enhanced by carbon based photo-sensitizers i.e. carbon nanodots (c-dots) and support i.e. reduced graphene oxide (graphene). The c-dots were prepared by electrophoretic dissolution of graphite electrode in water. The hydrothermal method was used to prepare the composite of graphene, c-dots and TiO2. These were characterized by XRD, TEM and Raman spectroscopy. The activity of the composites was judged by photocatalytic decoloration of methyl blue dye under visible light illumination. The enhanced photocatalytic activity has been attributed to the efficient photo-injection of electrons from c-dots to TiO2 along with superb ability of graphene to adsorb organic molecules as well electron sink from TiO2 for the photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Carbon nanodots
Electrophoretic dissolution
Ternary composite and dye decoloration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic measurements for methyl blue (MB) dye degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–visible absorption spectrum; (b) PL excitation and emission spectra recorded on aqueous dispersion of C-dots; (c) XRD; and (d) Representative TEM image recorded on the C-dots sample.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra (corresponding tau plot shown as inset); and (b) Powder XRDs recorded on TiO2, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2 samples. (c) Raman recorded on TiO2, C-dots, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2 samples. (d) Representative TEM image recorded on G-C-TiO2 composite sample.

MB decoloration rate (a) in the presence of only C-dots, graphene, TiO2 and in the absence of catalyst (b) in presence of TiO2, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2, under visible light illumination (100W/m2 tungsten-halogen lamp), where initial concentration of MB dye is 3ppm.

Comparison of rates of photocatalytic activity of TiO2, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2. From graph rate constants of the photo-decoloration of MB was calculated and tabulated in Table 1(Bar chart shown as inset).
Possible mechanism for electron transfer and photocatalytic decoloration of MB on G-C-TiO2 catalyst under visible light illumination.
Rate constants for photocatalytic MB decoloration on different catalyst samples.

head.ce:title: Metal free, carbon-TiO2 based composites for the visible light photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune 411 007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation & Photochemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene-carbon nanodots-TiO2 novel ternary composite was developed as visible light photocatalyst. Efficiency and visible light sensitivity of TiO2 for the photodecoloration were enhanced by carbon based photo-sensitizers i.e. carbon nanodots (c-dots) and support i.e. reduced graphene oxide (graphene). The c-dots were prepared by electrophoretic dissolution of graphite electrode in water. The hydrothermal method was used to prepare the composite of graphene, c-dots and TiO2. These were characterized by XRD, TEM and Raman spectroscopy. The activity of the composites was judged by photocatalytic decoloration of methyl blue dye under visible light illumination. The enhanced photocatalytic activity has been attributed to the efficient photo-injection of electrons from c-dots to TiO2 along with superb ability of graphene to adsorb organic molecules as well electron sink from TiO2 for the photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar photocatalytic degradation of hazardous bio-recalcitrant pollutants emerging from industrial effluents is gaining rapid intentness due to recent development of variety of photocatalysts. Many metal oxides specially TiO2 is tried extensively for this purpose, due to its robust photostability and being earth abundant based elements (Henderson, 2011; Nakata and Fujishima, 2012; Schneider et al., 2014; Tian et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2014). TiO2 based photocatalysts, however, suffer limitations and impede large-scale technological development due to (1) limited response in the visible light region and (2) dominant electron-hole recombination rate. Its, sensitivity towards visible region is extended by doping TiO2 with various metals or decorating its surface by metal complex based sensitizers (Kouamé et al., 2015; Miyauchi et al., 2016; Raza et al., 2015). The problem of recombination has been partially overcome by introducing metal nanoparticles islands on the TiO2 surface, which act as an electron-sink and help in fast separation of charge carriers (Kumar and Devi, 2011; Shet and Vidya, 2016; Swarnakar et al., 2013). However, the major limitation of this strategy is poor economic viability due to the use of expensive noble and precious metal decorations either in the form of metal complexes or metal nanoparticles. In recent, years immense interest has been developed to replace metal catalysts with metal-free carbon based variants (Ji et al., 2014; Leary and Westwood, 2011). Among them graphene analogs are at the top of the list because of (1) ease with this can be prepared on a large scale by chemical method (2) a better understanding of its structure and property relations. In photocatalytic applications, graphene and its analogs are preferred because of their high surface area, which helps in adsorption of hydrophobic organic pollutants. Graphene also has unique tendency to accept electrons from the semiconductor which helps in faster separation of charge carriers and thus reduce the recombination rate (Bell et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2012). Recently, an analogue of graphene viz. Carbon-nanodots (C-dots) has been demonstrated as stable visible light absorber and has the capacity to replace existing metal complex based sensitizers for TiO2 based photocatalysts (Miao et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015). C-dots have added advantages viz. effectiveness, stability, environmental and biocompatibility along with its wide ranging optical absorption covering visible region and up-converted PL behavior (Li et al., 2012; J. Liu et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). C-dots are nanometer sized carbon particles consisting of sp2 hybridized graphitic core functionalized with polar carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on the surface. Also, C-dots does not contain any toxic metals, therefore, viewed as most environmental friendly sensitizers (Lim et al., 2014; Miao et al., 2015). Ozin et al. has reported C-dots as sensitizers for nanocrystalline TiO2 solar cells (Mirtchev et al., 2012) and many reports presented the use of C-dots for visible light sensitization (Wang et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2013).

With this background, we hypothesized that if we prepare composite of TiO2 with graphene and C-dots, then this ternary composite is expected to deliver efficient electron-hole pair generation and separation. This property envisages extraordinary performance in the visible light photocatalysis. In literature, there are a few reports on such ternary composite of low band gap semiconductor and carbon materials with TiO2 (Lin et al., 2012; S. Liu et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2013; Xiao et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2012). Best of our knowledge, there is no report on ternary composite of TiO2 with graphene and C-dots and its applications for photocatalysis.

With this motivation, we prepared the graphene-C-dots-TiO2 (G-C-TiO2) composite by hydrothermal method. For that, C-dots were prepared by electrophoretic dissolution of graphite rods in aqueous medium. Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared by modified Hummers method. The photocatalytic properties of these composites have been studied by testing visible light decoloration of methyl blue (MB) as a model molecule for the pollutant. Our results brought out the unique property of the composite, which has a potential for the replacing the precious metal based existing catalyst conventionally used in photocatalysis.

Natural flake graphite (Loba Chem. Pvt. Ltd.), Sodium nitrate (Ranbaxy Fine Chem. Ltd.), Potassium permanganate (Merck), Graphite rod (Sigma Aldrich), Titanium isopropoxide (Sigma Aldrich), Methyl Blue (Sigma Aldrich) were used as received. All the solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water (conductivity<10−6 Scm−1).

C-dots dispersion was prepared by electrophoretic dissolution method. For that, two graphite rods were placed parallel to each other with a separation of 3cms in cell-reactor containing 150mL water. DC bias of 45V was applied between these electrodes for 200h, with continuous stirring. The anodic dissolution of graphite rod occurs, and the dark-yellow colored solution appeared gradually in the reactor. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 15,000–20,000rpm for 30min to remove the aggregated particles of carbon. Obtained solution was directly used as stock for the dispersion of C-dots (Ming et al., 2012).

GO samples were prepared by Hummers method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958), with a slight modification (Chadha et al., 2012; Markad et al., 2013). In brief, graphite powder (0.5g), sodium nitrate (0.5g) and sulfuric acid (23mL) were mixed in ice-bath under a continuous stirring. Potassium permanganate (3.0g) was slowly added to the reaction mixture at 20°C. The flask was then transferred to water bath (35±5°C), and a solution was stirred for an hr. to get a thick pasty product. 100mL water was added, and temperature of the bath was raised to 90±5°C under constant stirring for another 15min. The solution was diluted by adding 500mL water and 3mL H2O2 (30%) was subsequently added which led to color change from dark brown to yellow. The mixture was filtered and washed several times with hot water to eliminate the acid residue. The resultant solid was dried under vacuum and stored for subsequent use.

G-C-TiO2 composite was prepared by drop wise addition of Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4 (2mL dissolved in absolute alcohol with a ratio of 1:19) into mixture of graphene oxide and C-dots dispersion (110mL, 0.1mgmL−1), under stirred condition. After continuous stirring for 4h, a colloidal solution of G-C-TiO2 was formed. The resulting solution was then sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 48h. Obtained gray solid of G-C-TiO2 was washed with water, ethanol, and dried in a vacuum oven at 80°C. Control samples viz. graphene-TiO2 (G-TiO2), Carbon-nanodots-TiO2 (C-TiO2) and TiO2 were prepared without GO and C-dots by identical procedures.

UV–visible spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 diode array single beam spectrophotometer. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with the help of a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorimeter. Powder X-ray diffractograms (XRD) were recorded on the dried product using a Bruker, D8-Advance, X-ray diffractometer (Cu kα, 40kV, and 40mA). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded on the samples using a Technai G2 20 VTWIN transmission electron microscope (20–200kV), Raman spectra were recorded at room temperature at 633nm having laser beam focused with a spot size ∼1μm at power 10 mW. The Raman band of a silicon wafer at 520cm−1 was used to calibrate the spectrometer, and the accuracy of the spectral measurement was estimated to be better than 1cm−1. For the data collection, the dispersion of samples were drop casted on a glass slide and scattering was recorded at 180° having 50×LWD (long working distance) objective and CCD detector (Synapse, Horiba JobinYvon) based monochromator (LabRAM HR800, Horiba JobinYvon, France) together with an edge filter, covering a spectral range of 200–1800cm−1. BET surface areas of the powdered samples were estimated with the help of BET surface area analyzer (Autosorb-1, Quantachrome Instrument-ASIQM0001-5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic measurements for methyl blue (MB) dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: For photocatalytic decoloration studies 50W tungsten-halogen lamp mounted on the parabolic reflector and IR filter was employed. The light-beam was collimated and focused on the sample with suitable optics. The light intensity near the sample was measured to be 100W/m2. For the photocatalytic experiments, typically 20mg/100mL (Optimized concentration, Please refer Annexure A Fig. A1) dispersion of samples having 3ppmMB was used. The solution was firstly stirred in the dark for 20min to ensure the establishment of an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was then exposed to visible light. 3mL aliquots were withdrawn at certain time intervals, centrifuged and analyzed with the help of UV–Visible spectrophotometer and concentration of MB dye is estimated by measuring absorbance at 662nm.

Fig.1 (a) shows the UV–visible absorption spectrum recorded on as prepared aqueous dispersion of C-dots. A monotonous increase in the light absorption towards shorter wavelength having a brief shoulder near ∼305nm is seen. Fig.1(b) shows the photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra recorded for the same sample. It gives two excitation maxima at 372 and 467nm, the strong emission observed at 565nm warrants that particle have stable excited state, which is important for the sensitization application. Absorption and emission confirm the formation of C-dots (Li et al., 2012). Fig.1(c) shows the XRD pattern recorded on C-dots powder sample. A strong reflection at 24.01° (d=0.36nm) is indexed to (002) graphitic plane (JCPDS- 41-1487) and confirms the formation of desired product. We estimated particle size from XRD-FWHM by using Scherrer equation and it is 1.93nm. Fig.1(d) shows representative TEM image recorded for the C-dots samples ca. 2.0±0.2nm spherical structures legible in the micrograph (Refer Fig. A2 of Annexure A for size distribution curve). From all these results, the formation of C-dots is inferred.

Fig.2(a) shows UV–Visible absorption spectra recorded on TiO2, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2. From the tau plots (refer Fig.2 (a) inset), the apparent band gap of the composite samples have been estimated. The systematic decrease in apparent band gap value in the order TiO2, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2 samples is attributed to the interaction of reduced graphene oxide, and C-dots with TiO2. Fig.2(b) shows typical powder XRDs recorded on these samples. The prominent peaks are seen in all the diffractograms fitted faithfully in the anatase phase of TiO2 (JCPDS 84-1286). Expected graphitic peak of graphene and C-dots are not clearly seen in the composite samples (G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2). It is perhaps due to the overlapping of dominating peak from TiO2 at nearby positions (Markad et al., 2013). Fig.2(c) shows the Raman spectra recorded on TiO2, C-dots, G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2 samples. In case of C-dots and all the composite samples, D and G bands of graphitic structure are noted. Slight peak broadening in the TiO2 composite sample is attributed to the scattering of light from the TiO2 particles in the sample. Fig.2(d) shows the representative TEM image recorded on the G-C-TiO2 sample. Dark portions seen in the image indicates the formation of TiO2 particles on the graphene sheets. Calculated average particle size of TiO2 from TEM image is 5.16±0.16nm (Please refer Fig. A3 in Annexure A for size distribution curve), while particle size calculated for FWHM of XRD peak by using Scherrer equation is 6.23nm and size graphene sheets is in the few μm range (Please refer Fig. A4 of Annexure A). Enlarged image of G-C-TiO2 (refer right inset of Fig. 4(d)), shows black spot on the TiO2 surface which may be attributed to the decoration of C-dots on the surface of TiO2. In TEM SEAD pattern (Refer left inset of Fig.2(d)) the observed rings are fitted faithfully in the anatase phase of TiO2. Based on all the results, the formation of ternary composite of graphene, C-dots and TiO2 in which the C-dots are distributed uniformly on graphene sheets as well as on TiO2 is concluded.

The photocatalytic activity of composites has been tested by measuring rate of decoloration of MB under visible light illumination. Fig.3 (a) shows control measurements carried out in the presence of individual components of the composites viz. C-dots, graphene and TiO2 under the illumination.

There is insignificant change in concentration of MB after illumination for sufficiently longer time indicating inability of these components to act as photocatalyst individually in the visible light. The measurements have also been carried out on only MB solution which again shows insignificant change in its concentration and suggesting that MB molecule is stable in visible light. Fig.3(b) shows the similar measurements in the presence of G-TiO2, C-TiO2 and G-C-TiO2. The plot recorded earlier in the presence of TiO2 is superimposed in this figure for comparison. The slight decolourization of MB is observed in case of G-TiO2 as compared to that of TiO2 alone, which could be partly due to the adsorption of MB molecule on graphene surface (Han et al., 2012). 100% decoloration of MB in case of C-TiO2 was noted within ca. 120min under visible light illumination. This feature of C-TiO2 sample is attributed to the role of C-dots as a sensitizer (Li et al., 2012; Mirtchev et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2013) having absorption maxima in visible region (Please refer Scheme 1). Further, the MB decoloration was studied by incorporating graphene in the C-TiO2 system to contemplate its role not only as good adsorbent but also as an electron sink. As anticipated G-C-TiO2 had shown 100% decoloration of MB in ca. 90min with nearly two fold increase in the decoloration rate (please refer Table 1 ) as compared to C-TiO2 system. This observation is attributed to the (i) increase in surface area of G-C-TiO2 sample (please refer Annexure A Table A1) (Bell et al., 2011) which facilitates more adsorption of MB molecules at graphene surface. This property consequently leads to the residual vacancies on TiO2 surface due to slip induced surface diffusion. The organic molecules get oxidized by the holes directly or by the photoactive radicals, generated in the photocatalytic reactions to finally get mineralized into CO2, H2O and mineral acids (Kale and Thakur, 2015). (ii) π-conjugated structure of graphene acts as an excellent electron sink (Sher Shah et al., 2012). Moreover, the favorable positions of conduction band minimum of TiO2 and work function (W.F.) of graphene helps efficient interfacial transfer of electrons from TiO2 to graphene leading to enhanced separation of electron-hole pairs, ultimately resulting in increased photocatalytic activity (Lightcap et al., 2010).

A plausible mechanism for electron transfer and photocatalytic decoloration of MB on G-C-TiO2 catalyst under visible light illumination is shown in Scheme 1 Where, C-dots act as visible light sensitizer, which can transfer electron to the TiO2. Also, in literature, it is reported that due to unconverted PL behavior, C-dots has the ability to transfer energy to TiO2 which helps to generate electron-hole pair in TiO2. These excited electrons from TiO2 surface can be transferred to graphene surface where graphene acts as electron trap. These electrons from graphene surface reduce oxygen to reactive oxygen. Electron accepting ability of graphene surface helps to reduce electron-hole pair recombination in case of TiO2. Photogenerated electrons on the TiO2 surface also can react with dissolved oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species. Collectively, reactive oxygen species formed on graphene surface and TiO2 surface react with water which eventually produces hydroxyl radical for mineralization of MB. On the other hand holes on the TiO2 surface react with water to form hydroxyl radical which eventually helps in mineralization of dye or holes can directly react with dye molecule and mineralize the dye molecule directly (Kumar and Devi, 2011; Sher Shah et al., 2012).

Photo decoloration of MB fitted faithfully in pseudo-first order kinetics as shown in Fig. 4 . From this plot, first order rate constants for MB decoloration for various photocatalysts were calculated (Ovhal et al., 2013) and shown as bar chart in Fig. 4. Obtained results show higher value of rate constant for G-C-TiO2 sample than that of C-TiO2 and TiO2.

For first time, preparation of ternary composite made up of graphene, C-dots and TiO2 (G-C-TiO2) by hydrothermal method is reported. The materials were thoroughly characterized by XRD, Raman, TEM, BET, UV–vis, steady state PL and SAED. The visible light photocatalysis by these composites were monitored by decoloration of MB. From our results, a dual role of C-dots and graphene as photo sensitizer and electron sink respectively are brought out. We believe that this ternary composite would be best alternative to the conventional metal based photocatalysts for the clean environment and other energy harvesting applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to BRNS-DAE for financial support. G.B.M. thanks BARC-SPPU Collaborative Ph.D. Program for the financial support. Authors are thankful to the Department of Physics, Savitribai Phule Pune University, for the use of the XRD and TEM facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2016.12.025.
